title: Chapter 32: The Night Before
content: The ceiling stares back at me and I stare back at it and neither of us blinks.

Rowan's penthouse is forty floors above the city, and from this bed you can feel Manhattan vibrating through the glass — a low, persistent hum that lives in the bones of the building the way the other hum lives in mine. The room is dark except for the thin blade of light slipping under the bathroom door where neither of us bothered to turn off the vanity lamp. The air carries traces of cedar from the closet, something woody and expensive that clings to his sheets, and beneath it the faint mineral smell of rain that hasn't fallen yet.
I should be sleeping.

Tomorrow is the showcase. Tomorrow is the runway and the lights and the eyes and Maren, who has been circling for weeks like something that smells blood. Tomorrow is the thing I've been building toward and running from in equal measure, and my body knows it — every cell taut, the hum a restless current under my skin, as if my bones are rehearsing for a performance I haven't agreed to.
Rowan is beside me. Also awake. Also staring at the same ceiling.
The silence between us is the kind that only exists before something irrevocable. The held-breath silence. The silence of a wire pulled so tight it sings.
"Whatever happens tomorrow —"
"Don't."
The word comes out sharper than I intend. I feel him turn his head toward me even though I'm still looking up.
"I need to say it."
"Then say it."

I don't know what I expect. Some qualified reassurance, maybe. Some careful hedge wrapped in the language of loyalty that leaves him an exit route. I've heard those before. I've memorized the architecture of them — the way they sound like devotion but function like doors left ajar.
What I don't expect is certainty.

"Whatever happens tomorrow — the showcase, Maren, any of it — I am not leaving. I am not retreating. And I am not afraid of what you become." His voice is steady and unhurried in the dark, each sentence laid down like a brick. "I want you to hear that from me, not just assume it."
I turn my head to look at him.

In the half-dark his profile is all sharp edges and soft eyes — the jaw that could have been carved from the same marble as the buildings he designs, the cheekbone catching what little light there is, but his eyes are open and unguarded in a way that his face never is in daylight. As if darkness is the only condition under which he lets himself be read.
"You keep saying that," I tell him. "And I keep not believing it. Not because of you. Because of everyone before you."

The words land in the space between us and I wish I could take them back, not because they're untrue but because they're too true — a confession I didn't mean to make out loud, and now it's there, laying bare the architecture of my distrust like a blueprint spread across the bed.
Rowan is quiet for a long time. The city hums.
"Tell me about them," he says. "The ones who left."

Something seizes in my chest. The hum sharpens, or maybe I just notice it more. I taste salt on my tongue — tears I haven't cried, won't cry, have spent four centuries learning not to cry because crying is a luxury and I have never been able to afford it.
"Why?"
"Because you've been carrying them alone for four hundred years. And I'd like to share the weight, if you'll let me."

I almost laugh. I almost say no. I almost do a lot of things. But the dark makes liars of us in different directions — it makes me more honest, not less, and the honest truth is that I want to tell him. I want someone to know.

"There was a priest," I begin. "In Seville. 1598, something like that — the years blur. Father Alonso. He was kind, at first. Gave me bread when I was starving in the alley behind his church. Taught me to read, a little. I was staying in the vestry, sleeping on a pallet of old vestments." I pause. The memory has a smell — incense and sour wool. "One morning I woke up and I'd shifted in the night. My face had changed. My hands. He came in with the bread and saw me and dropped the loaf on the floor and did not pick it up. He called me a divine punishment. Said God had sent me to test his faith." A breath. "He passed the test. He threw me out."
Rowan's hand finds mine in the dark. His thumb traces across my knuckles, slow, unhurried. He doesn't say anything. He just holds on.

"There was a doctor in Edinburgh. 1702. Dr. Crowe. He was — curious. That was the word he used. Curious. He measured me. My skull, my hands, the angle of my jaw before and after. He wrote it all down in a journal with a steel nib pen, and I let him because I thought curiosity was the same as care." My voice goes thin. "It wasn't. He tried to catalog me like a specimen. Like a butterfly under glass. When I refused to shift on command, he lost interest. Told his colleagues at the university about the anomaly he'd studied, past tense, like I was already dead."
Rowan's thumb has not stopped moving. It's the smallest thing and the steadiest — a metronome keeping me in the present while my voice drifts through the past.
"Then there was William."
His hand tightens, just slightly. He's heard me say the name once before. He knows the weight of it.
"William was the worst," I say, "because William loved me first and then didn't. And there's no cruelty like the cruelty of someone who used to be kind."
I close my eyes. I can still see him. I can always still see him — the lamp-lit room in the Boston townhouse, the way his face changed before he even had words for what he was feeling.

"William held my hand while I was still Ora. Brushed the hair back from my forehead. Told me I was the most beautiful woman he'd ever seen." I open my eyes again and the dark is the same dark, unchanged, which is the only mercy it offers. "Then I shifted. My jaw changed. My hands changed. I looked like his brother instead of his lover. And he looked at me like I'd betrayed him. Like my body was a lie I'd told."

The word *lie* sits in the air, ugly and accurate, the shape every accusation has taken for four hundred years. My throat is tight and the salt taste is stronger now, the tears I refuse to let fall sitting somewhere behind my sternum like a stone in a river.

Rowan's hand moves from mine. I think for one wild second that the story has done it — that the weight of my history is exactly what I said it was, a reason, and he's pulling away. But he doesn't. He lifts his hand to my face, and his fingers find the wetness at the corner of my eye that I didn't even know was there, and he wipes it away with a touch so gentle it almost breaks me.
"Your body isn't a lie," he says. "It's a truth that shifts."

I stare at him. The shape of the sentence is so precise, so *right*, that for a moment I can't breathe. Every person who has ever looked at me has seen the shift as deception. The priest saw sin. The doctor saw anomaly. William saw betrayal. No one — not once, not in four centuries — has looked at what I am and called it *truth*.
"That's very poetic," I manage.
"I've been practicing."

And I laugh — but it comes out wrong, a wet, broken sound that is half sob and doesn't fool either of us. Rowan doesn't flinch. He doesn't try to fix it. He lies there in the dark with his hand still on my face and lets me be as messy as I need to be, and that might be the most intimate thing anyone has ever done.
The laughter dissolves. The silence returns, but it's different now — not the wire-pulled-tight silence of before but something softer, something that holds space.
I say the hardest thing.
"What if I shift tomorrow? On the runway? In front of everyone?"

The question has been living in my chest for weeks, coiled and waiting. I've been carrying it like a stone in my pocket, running my fingers over it, turning it over, feeling it get heavier. The showcase is the most exposed I've ever been. Every eye, every camera, every person who has ever whispered about the mysterious Orlando Leclair and what she might really be. And the hum is louder every day, restless, as if it knows something I don't.
Rowan doesn't hesitate.
"Then I'll walk onto that runway and stand beside you while it happens."
"Your empire —"

"Can be rebuilt." His voice is iron. Not the cold iron of a man defending a fortress, but the hot iron of a man who has chosen. "You can't. And I won't let you face that alone." He shifts onto his side to face me fully, and I can feel the weight of his attention like sunlight. "I've spent my whole life protecting things that don't matter. Buildings. Companies. Reputations." He reaches out and places his hand flat over my heart, and the warmth of his palm is so immediate, so present, that it pulls me out of the past and into my body like a rope thrown into deep water. "You matter. This matters."

I press his hand harder against my chest. I want him to feel it — the heartbeat and the hum tangled together, inseparable, one of them mine and one of them older than mine and both of them proof that I am here, that I am alive, that whatever I am is still breathing.

My heart is pounding. Or maybe that's the hum. I can't tell anymore and I don't want to, because the distinction has never mattered less. What matters is his palm against my sternum. What matters is that he hasn't moved. What matters is that when I said *what if I shift in front of everyone* his first instinct was not to strategize or minimize or plan damage control — it was to say *I'll stand beside you*.

The room is quiet. The city hums forty floors below, the sound of ten million lives running in parallel, each one certain of its own shape. I am not certain of mine. I have never been certain of mine. But for the first time the uncertainty doesn't feel like a threat. It feels like a thread — something that could be woven.
"I have loved you in velvet," I say, "and I have loved you in silk. And I will love you when my bones turn to stardust."
Rowan's breath catches. I feel it against my face, the small hitch of someone who was not expecting to be hit.
"That's from a letter I wrote in 1743," I tell him. "But I mean it now more than I did then. Because now I have something to lose."

The words hang in the cedar-scented dark. I have said them. I cannot unsay them. And maybe that's the point — maybe trust is not a feeling but a burning of ships, a closing of doors, a choice made once and then made again every day until it becomes the ground you stand on.
Rowan pulls me close.

His arms go around me and I go into them, and we fit together the way we always do — imperfectly, which is to say, perfectly. His chin rests on the crown of my head and my face is pressed against his chest and I can hear his heartbeat, slow and steady, the opposite of mine. He is not afraid. He is not afraid of me. He is not afraid of what I might become by morning.

Below us, the city hums — a ten-million-voiced chord of traffic and breath and light. Inside me, the hum hums — ancient and restless and patient. And the two sounds weave together, the way fabric weaves, threads of different origins crossing and undercrossing and locking into something stronger than either one alone.

I think about the priest. I think about the doctor. I think about William, who held my hand and then let go. I think about every door that closed and every road that narrowed and every time I told myself that love was a country I'd been exiled from.

And then I think about this: a man in the dark with his hand on my heart, telling me my shifting body is not a lie but a truth. Telling me he'll walk onto a runway. Telling me I matter. Telling me *this* matters.

Tomorrow is the showcase. The battlefield. The lights and the eyes and Maren's calculated smile across a room. Tomorrow I will stand on that runway in whatever body I'm wearing and whatever body I become, and the world will see what it sees, and afterward nothing will be the same.

But tonight — tonight I am here, in cedar and dark, in the arms of someone who chose me without conditions. Tonight the hum and the heartbeat are indistinguishable. Tonight I am not weighing exits or planning retreats or rehearsing the face I'll make when he leaves, because he keeps not leaving.
Tomorrow will come. It always does. The shift will come, or it won't. Maren will make her move, or she won't. The eyes will see, and the cameras will capture, and the world will have its opinion.
But for the first time in four hundred years, I am not facing tomorrow alone.
And that — that is the bravest thing I know how to be.
